title: Listening - Part 2
description: You will hear a man called Leo talking about a local film festival. For questions 9–18, complete the sentences with a word or short phrase.
instructions: You will hear a man called Leo talking about a local film festival. For questions 9–18, complete the sentences with a word or short phrase.

extracts.context: A man called Leo talks about helping at a local film festival.
extracts.audio_script: Good evening. I’m Leo and I organise volunteers for the Riverside Film Festival, an annual event where audiences can watch films and meet people who make them. The festival began because our town lost its final cinema. People occasionally say the building was turned into a supermarket, but it actually became a [9]furniture shop[/9]. A group of residents therefore began showing films in different community spaces, and the festival grew from that small idea. We now screen around forty films over one week in September.||We use four venues. The large theatre is best for popular evening films, while short daytime films are shown in a university room. The location audiences say they most enjoy is the old [10]town hall[/10], where films are projected onto a large screen in a beautiful upstairs room. Volunteers work in all venues, helping people find seats, checking tickets and answering questions. New volunteers do not start by dealing with ticket problems. Their first role is generally handing out printed [11]festival guides[/11] at the entrances and explaining where people can find programme information.||No previous experience is needed, but everybody attends one short meeting beforehand. We used to spend most of it explaining film titles, which was not especially useful. Now its main purpose is to explain [12]customer care[/12], as visitors may need clear information when venues are busy or screenings are delayed. During shifts, all volunteers wear a badge and a blue top supplied by us. They should bring one small personal item: a [13]water bottle[/13], because a shift can last three hours and venues become warm when full. Free drinking water is available throughout the festival.||Many visitors come to hear guests speak after screenings. This year's best-known guest is not an actor, as some newspapers first reported. She is a [14]costume designer[/14] who has worked on several famous historical films and will discuss how clothing helps tell a story. We are also inviting young filmmakers to take part. They can submit a film lasting no longer than ten minutes, and the winning entry will receive [15]editing equipment[/15], donated by a local media company. All submitted films must be the students' own work.||Volunteers are thanked in several ways. Everybody working two or more shifts receives a pair of [16]cinema tickets[/16] for a local independent cinema in the next town. Some volunteers may also assist at our schools' event, where children learn how to create [17]sound effects[/17] for a short animation using ordinary household objects. This has always been one of the liveliest sessions. Finally, the festival supports a local cause. Last year's donations provided books for a children's centre; this year the money will be spent on new [18]wheelchair access[/18] at one of our venues. Application forms are on our website, and the final date for volunteers to register is the end of July.

dialogue.speaker: leo
dialogue.text: Good evening. I’m Leo and I organise volunteers for the Riverside Film Festival, an annual event where audiences can watch films and meet people who make them. The festival began because our town lost its final cinema. People occasionally say the building was turned into a supermarket, but it actually became a furniture shop. A group of residents therefore began showing films in different community spaces, and the festival grew from that small idea. We now screen around forty films over one week in September.||We use four venues. The large theatre is best for popular evening films, while short daytime films are shown in a university room. The location audiences say they most enjoy is the old town hall, where films are projected onto a large screen in a beautiful upstairs room. Volunteers work in all venues, helping people find seats, checking tickets and answering questions. New volunteers do not start by dealing with ticket problems. Their first role is generally handing out printed festival guides at the entrances and explaining where people can find programme information.||No previous experience is needed, but everybody attends one short meeting beforehand. We used to spend most of it explaining film titles, which was not especially useful. Now its main purpose is to explain customer care, as visitors may need clear information when venues are busy or screenings are delayed. During shifts, all volunteers wear a badge and a blue top supplied by us. They should bring one small personal item: a water bottle, because a shift can last three hours and venues become warm when full. Free drinking water is available throughout the festival.||Many visitors come to hear guests speak after screenings. This year's best-known guest is not an actor, as some newspapers first reported. She is a costume designer who has worked on several famous historical films and will discuss how clothing helps tell a story. We are also inviting young filmmakers to take part. They can submit a film lasting no longer than ten minutes, and the winning entry will receive editing equipment, donated by a local media company. All submitted films must be the students' own work.||Volunteers are thanked in several ways. Everybody working two or more shifts receives a pair of cinema tickets for a local independent cinema in the next town. Some volunteers may also assist at our schools' event, where children learn how to create sound effects for a short animation using ordinary household objects. This has always been one of the liveliest sessions. Finally, the festival supports a local cause. Last year's donations provided books for a children's centre; this year the money will be spent on new wheelchair access at one of our venues. Application forms are on our website, and the final date for volunteers to register is the end of July.

questions.question: The town's last cinema was converted into a (9) .
questions.explanation: Leo explains that the former cinema became a 'furniture shop'.

questions.question: The venue preferred by audiences is the old (10) .
questions.explanation: Leo says audiences most enjoy screenings in the old 'town hall'.

questions.question: New volunteers usually begin by giving out (11) .
questions.explanation: Leo states that newcomers first hand out 'festival guides'.

questions.question: The main subject of the volunteer meeting is now (12) .
questions.explanation: Leo says the meeting now mainly explains 'customer care'.

questions.question: Volunteers are advised to take a (13) during shifts.
questions.explanation: Leo tells volunteers to bring a 'water bottle'.

questions.question: This year's best-known guest works as a (14) .
questions.explanation: Leo clarifies that the guest is a 'costume designer', not an actor.

questions.question: The prize for the winning young filmmaker is (15) .
questions.explanation: Leo says the winner will receive 'editing equipment'.

questions.question: Volunteers completing at least two shifts receive (16) .
questions.explanation: Leo says volunteers who do two shifts obtain 'cinema tickets'.

questions.question: At the schools' event, children will learn to make (17) .
questions.explanation: Leo explains that children will create 'sound effects'.

questions.question: Festival donations this year will pay for improved (18) .
questions.explanation: Leo states that donations will fund new 'wheelchair access'.
